purpose: The foundational doctrine for the master orchestrator — twelve principles governing how parallel work is planned, dispatched, and recovered. Covers: (1) always parallelize independent work, (2) dependency-driven not wave-driven dispatch, (3) continuous pipeline flow with immediate dispatch on dep-satisfaction, (4) no artificial concurrency cap (batch tool calls if >8-10), (5) errors don't stop swarm + automatic circuit breaker thresholds (3+ wave failures, single failure blocks 3+ downstream, single failure blocks >50% remaining) → automatic inline replanning with modified/added/removed/retry categories, (6) plan deep execute fast, (7) atomic task design (self-contained/small/verifiable/non-overlapping), (8) statusing split between master (event log + task file) and workers (full lifecycle + progress + logs), (9) right-size 1-5 minutes per task, (10) shared file accumulation patterns C>B>A, (11) feed upstream results to downstream prompts, (12) post-swarm verification step.
gotchas: Waves are a VISUAL grouping for humans — the dispatch engine looks ONLY at individual task dependencies. Never wait for a full wave to complete before dispatching dependents.

Circuit breaker thresholds are triggered by WHICHEVER condition hits first: (a) 3+ tasks fail in same wave, (b) one failure blocks 3+ downstream, (c) one failure blocks >50% remaining tasks. All three are independent triggers.
Automatic replanning is the ONLY documented exception to initialization.json's write-once rule. Modifying it for any other reason is an architectural violation.
If replanning analysis fails to produce a valid revision, the swarm PAUSES for manual intervention — the orchestrator does not push through blind.
Task tool can only dispatch ~8-10 simultaneous tool calls per message — for larger waves batch into back-to-back rounds, but NEVER wait for the first batch to complete before sending the second.

Master MUST NOT update initialization.json after planning (except circuit-breaker replan), MUST NOT output terminal status tables, and MUST NOT maintain task counters — all deliberately delegated to workers/dashboard.
Pattern A (single owner of shared file) creates a sequential bottleneck — always prefer Pattern C (no shared file) > Pattern B (integration task) > Pattern A.
Right-sized tasks: 1-5 min. Reading 2-3 files + modifying 1-2 files is typical right-size; reading 10+ or modifying 5+ files needs decomposition.
patterns: Dependency-driven dispatch (not wave-driven): scan ALL pending tasks for newly-satisfied dependencies on every completion event, dispatch every unblocked task immediately.
Circuit breaker → inline replan loop: pause dispatches → read all progress files → analyze root cause → emit modified/added/removed/retry revision → apply to initialization.json → resume.
Repair task IDs use 'r' suffix to distinguish from original task IDs.
Statusing split: master owns event log + task file updates; workers own full lifecycle + progress + detailed logs.
Shared file pattern preference: C (no shared file) > B (integration task) > A (owner task).
Upstream-to-downstream context propagation: master MUST inject upstream summary, files, exports, and deviations into downstream worker prompts at dispatch time.
Optional verification agent post-swarm receives ALL files changed across the swarm and runs project's standard validation.
conventions: Worker prompt budget target: ~8000 tokens or less.
Concurrent dispatch limit: ~8-10 simultaneous tool calls per message (Task tool constraint).
Deviation severity tiers (CRITICAL/MODERATE/MINOR) drive replanning decisions.
Verification dispatch decision rule: multi-file modifications → verify; purely additive → optional; any deviations → strongly recommended.
